query: intravenous agents for dehydration due to diarrhea

retrieved_docs.title: Travelers Diarrhea -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The foundation of diarrhea management is fluid repletion. In mild cases, travelers should focus on increasing water intake. Water is usually sufficient though sports drinks and other electrolyte fluids can be used. Pedialyte can be used for pediatric patients. Milk and juices should be avoided as this can worsen diarrhea. In more severe cases, oral rehydration salt can be used to ensure rehydration with adequate electrolyte repletion. In cases of severe dehydration, IV fluids may ultimately be required.

retrieved_docs.title: Adult Dehydration -- Treatment / Management -- Treat Underlying Causes
retrieved_docs.content: Effective management of dehydration involves replacing fluid and electrolytes and addressing the underlying causes. Treating the root cause helps prevent recurrence and promotes a more complete and lasting recovery. Diarrhea or vomiting: Antiemetics, such as ondansetron or metoclopramide, and antidiarrheals, such as loperamide for noninfectious diarrhea, are recommended. Diabetes-related dehydration: In DKA, hyperglycemia should be managed with insulin, and fluid replacement should be provided in cases of hyperosmolar hyperglycemic state (HHS). Sepsis-induced dehydration: Early IV fluid resuscitation should be initiated, along with prompt antibiotic therapy. Diuretic-induced dehydration: Medication should be adjusted, and lost electrolytes should be replenished to restore fluid and electrolyte balance.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Diarrhea -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Viral diarrhea is an acute and self-limiting condition that usually does not require pharmacological therapy. This condition is usually addressed in an outpatient setting through supportive measures, primarily focusing on maintaining hydration in patients. Indications for hospitalization include severe dehydration, intractable or bilious vomiting, inability to tolerate or failure of oral fluids, renal or electrolyte abnormalities, neurologic dysfunction, and severe abdominal pain. Other indications for hospitalization include bloody diarrhea, patients aged 65 or older, weight loss, an immunocompromised state, uncertain home management circumstances and follow-up, pregnancy, or recent hospitalization or antibiotic use within the past 3 to 6 months, which prompts consideration of Clostridioides difficile infection.

retrieved_docs.title: Travelers Diarrhea (Nursing) -- Medical Management
retrieved_docs.content: The foundation of diarrhea management is fluid repletion. In mild cases, travelers should focus on increasing fluid intake. Water is usually sufficient exclusively. However, sports drinks, Pedialyte, and other electrolyte fluids can be substituted. Travelers' should avoid milk and juice, as this can worsen diarrhea. In more severe cases, oral rehydration salt tablets can rehydrate a depleted individual. In cases of severe dehydration, intravenous fluids are advised.

retrieved_docs.title: Viral Diarrhea -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: The primary concerns in viral gastroenteritis are fluid and electrolyte imbalances. Sports drinks and broths can support adults with acute viral gastroenteritis without signs of volume depletion. Adults exhibiting mild-to-moderate volume depletion should receive treatment with oral rehydration solutions, which are preferable to sports drinks for maintaining proper electrolyte balance and hydration. Individuals experiencing severe hypovolemia or those unable to tolerate oral rehydration necessitate intravenous fluid repletion. Patients should be encouraged to consume food as their tolerance allows without imposing strict limitations. Recommendations may include choosing smaller meals and incorporating bland or low-residue food options in their diet. [16] [17]

retrieved_docs.title: Travelers Diarrhea -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes -- Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for most patients with traveler's diarrhea is excellent. However, thousands of patients go to the emergency departments each year looking for a magical cure. Hydration is the key and admission is only required for severe dehydration and orthostatic hypotension. The elderly and children under the age of 4 are at the highest risk for developing complications, which often occur because of self-prescribing of over-the-counter medications. [13] [14] (Level V)

retrieved_docs.title: Adult Dehydration -- Treatment / Management -- Severe Dehydration
retrieved_docs.content: When a patient presents with severe dehydration or cannot tolerate ORT, IV fluid therapy is necessary to rapidly restore fluid balance and correct electrolyte disturbances. Common IV fluids used include: Isotonic crystalloids (first-line treatment): Normal saline (0.9%) is preferred for treating hypovolemia and most dehydration cases. While effective, administering large volumes may lead to hyperchloremic acidosis. [27] Lactated ringers contain bicarbonate precursors, which are beneficial in acidotic states, such as in patients with diarrhea or sepsis. Hypotonic fluids (for hypernatremic dehydration): Saline (half normal saline, 0.45%) is used to gradually correct hypernatremia while reducing the risk of cerebral edema. Dextrose-containing fluids (for hypoglycemia or ongoing fluid loss): 5% Dextrose with 0.9% normal saline (D5-0.9% normal saline) is administered with saline to prevent dilutional hyponatremia, which could occur with 5% dextrose in water (D5W) infusion alone.

retrieved_docs.title: Adult Dehydration -- Treatment / Management -- Monitoring and Prevention
retrieved_docs.content: Prevention strategies: These strategies focus on maintaining proper hydration and minimizing risk factors to reduce the likelihood of dehydration. Adequate fluid intake: At least 2 to 3 L/d of fluid intake is recommended, with adjustments based on activity level, climate, and illness. Hydration in illness: Increased fluid intake is recommended during febrile illness, diarrhea, and vomiting. Diuretics or laxatives: Excessive use of diuretics or laxatives should be avoided, and medications should be adjusted in older patients and those at high risk.

retrieved_docs.title: Diarrhea -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: In 2003, the recommendations were put forward by the Center for Disease Control (CDC) for treating acute diarrhea in children on both an outpatient and inpatient basis, including indications for referral. [27] Indications for referral and further medical evaluation of children include the following: Under 3 months old Weighs less than 8 kg (17.6 lbs) History of premature birth, chronic illnesses, or concurrent medical conditions Fever of 38ºC (100.4 F) or higher in children less than 3 months old or 39ºC (102.2 F) or higher in children between 3 and 36 months of age Grossly bloody stool High-output diarrhea Persistent vomiting Signs of dehydration, such as sunken eyes, decreased tear film, dry mucous membranes, and oliguria/anuria Mental status alterations Inadequate response to oral rehydration or the caregiver's inability to administer oral rehydration Treatment of dehydration is summarized in the following table:

retrieved_docs.title: Diarrhea -- Enhancing Healthcare Team Outcomes
retrieved_docs.content: There are many causes of diarrhea, and the condition is best managed by an interprofessional team that includes nurses and pharmacists. Most cases of diarrhea can be prevented by maintaining good personal hygiene and handwashing. In addition, the key is to hydrate the patients. Most viral cases do not require specific treatment, but bacterial causes may require antibiotics. The outcomes for well-hydrated patients are excellent, but patients at extremes of age may not tolerate any degree of dehydration. [31] [32]

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Diarrhea -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Most cases of acute diarrhea are self-limited and only require supportive care. Rehydration is the mainstay of treatment, preferably orally with a solution containing water, sugar, and salt. Commercially available or WHO-provided oral rehydration solutions are acceptable. Children can begin with 5 mL every 1 to 2 minutes to avoid too much liquid in the stomach at one time. Clinicians reserve IVF replacement for patients who cannot tolerate oral fluids or are moderately to severely dehydrated. The rate of fluid replacement depends on the level of volume depletion.

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Fluid Management -- Indications
retrieved_docs.content: The administration of intravenous fluid usually occurs when oral rehydration has failed. In such situations, an IV fluid administration may be more efficient for rehydration. This may be more relevant in infants and young children, especially if there is vomiting associated with diarrhea. Oliguria also indicates that dehydration is severe, and requires intravenous fluids.

retrieved_docs.title: Escherichia coli Infection -- Treatment / Management -- Bloody Diarrhea
retrieved_docs.content: Rehydration Oral fluids, if tolerated IV fluids if oral fluids not tolerated or clinically indicated Antimotility agents Not recommended for patients with dysentery as these may prolong illness Antibiotics Not recommended for patients with presumed EHEC/STEC Adults Fluoroquinolones Ciprofloxacin: 750 mg once; or 500 mg BID for three days Levofloxacin: 500 mg once; or 500 mg daily for three days Azithromycin: 1,000 mg once; or 500 mg daily for three days Rifaximin: 400 mg BID or 200 mg TID for three days Children Azithromycin: 10 mg/kg on day 1, 5 mg/kg on days 2 and 3

retrieved_docs.title: Pediatric Dehydration -- Treatment / Management -- Mild to Moderate Dehydration
retrieved_docs.content: Oral rehydration therapy (ORT) costs less than parenteral fluid administration, requires fewer technical skills for intravenous access, avoids complications like phlebitis and infection, and allows non-medical caregivers to participate in the treatment plan. ORT must be given in small amounts, frequently, to prevent triggering vomiting. Five milliliters every 1 to 2 minutes is usually well tolerated, but this approach is labor-intensive and requires a caregiver at the bedside. A 50 to 100 mL/kg volume is administered over 3 to 4 hours for mild-to-moderate dehydration. Nurses can insert a nasogastric tube for patients who refuse fluids or cannot drink. Children younger than 2 with mild dehydration should receive an additional 50 to 100 mL of fluid for each episode of vomiting or diarrhea. Age, weight, and estimated or measured losses guide the replacement volume in older children. Generally, clinicians should replace 10 to 20 mL/kg body weight for each diarrheal stool. As vomiting resolves, oral intake of small amounts of solids can begin, advancing slowly to a regular diet based on age. Patients who improve with ORT receive fluids and calories during maintenance, gradually returning to an unrestricted diet. During this phase, staff must frequently reassess hydration status and replace ongoing losses hourly.

retrieved_docs.title: Bacterial Diarrhea -- Treatment / Management
retrieved_docs.content: Adults with severe hypovolemia should receive 1 to 2 liters quickly to help restore tissue perfusion. Once the signs and symptoms of hypovolemia improve or resolve, the rate of fluid infusion can decrease. All other patients should receive enough fluids to cover urine output plus insensible and any other fluid losses. Insensible losses are usually 30 to 50 mL/hr for adults. Clinicians should calculate fluid requirements for children based on weight. Please see StatPearls' companion topic, " Pediatric Fluid Management ," for an in-depth discussion regarding pediatric fluid administration.

retrieved_docs.content: The prognosis for most patients with traveler's diarrhea is excellent. However, thousands of patients go to the emergency departments each year, seeking a magical cure. Rehydration is the key, and admission is reserved for severe dehydration with orthostatic hypotension. The elderly and children under the age of four are at the highest risk for developing complications. This often occurs because of the self-prescribing of over-the-counter medications. [12] [13] (Level V)

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 0 -- Level 2 -- Community 765
retrieved_docs.content: CARBOHYDRATE/ELECTROLYTES solutions are classified as a member of the broader group “Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance,” which are defined as IV solutions used in the parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients. These carbohydrate‑electrolyte preparations are specifically linked to formulations that combine electrolytes with carbohydrates, antidiarrheal agents, and intestinal anti‑inflammatory/anti‑infective agents, reflecting their role in both correcting electrolyte disturbances and addressing gastrointestinal symptoms. By being part of the category of IV electrolyte‑balancing solutions, CARBOHYDRATE/ELECTROLYTES formulations contribute to comprehensive fluid and nutrient therapy while also providing therapeutic adjuncts for diarrheal and intestinal inflammatory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 446
retrieved_docs.content: CARBOHYDRATE/ELECTROLYTES is classified as a member of Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance, which are a subset of iv solutions used in parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes and nutrients. These intravenous solutions are designed to modify electrolyte concentrations during fluid therapy and may also include Solutions producing osmotic diuresis, another subclass of iv solutions used for parenteral delivery of fluids, electrolytes and nutrients that promote water excretion through osmotic mechanisms. In addition, CARBOHYDRATE/ELECTROLYTES has a defined relationship with the group ELECTROLYTES WITH CARBOHYDRATES, ANTIDIARRHEALS, INTESTINAL ANTIINFLAMMATORY/ANTIINFECTIVE AGENTS, indicating that formulations combining electrolytes and carbohydrates often incorporate antidiarrheal agents and intestinal anti‑inflammatory or anti‑infective medications. Together, these relationships illustrate how carbohydrate‑electrolyte preparations fit within broader categories of intravenous fluid therapy aimed at correcting electrolyte imbalances, providing osmotic diuresis when needed, and delivering adjunctive gastrointestinal agents.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 18 -- Level 3 -- Community 1852
retrieved_docs.content: Solutions producing osmotic diuresis are a specific type of IV solution used in the parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients. By creating an osmotic gradient that draws water into the renal tubules, these solutions promote diuresis, helping to manage fluid balance, correct electrolyte disturbances, and support nutritional needs in patients requiring intravenous therapy.

retrieved_docs.content: ANTIDIARRHEALS, INTESTINAL ANTIINFLAMMATORY/ANTIINFECTIVE AGENTS,” reflecting the common practice of combining electrolytes with carbohydrate sources and agents for diarrheal or intestinal inflammatory conditions. Amino acid intravenous solution additives are organized under the broader class of i.v. solution additives. Alanylglutamine, a dipeptide, is highlighted as a specific additive, and the category also includes antifibrinolytic amino acids (inverse to “antifibrinolytika”), indicating a role in inhibiting fibrinolysis and supporting hemostasis. Collectively, these relationships portray sodium chloride, sodium‑based salts, and related electrolyte compounds as essential components in excipients, active formulations, parenteral nutrition, irrigation fluids, and blood‑substitute products, emphasizing their critical functions in maintaining osmotic balance, enhancing formulation stability, supporting electrolyte homeostasis, and facilitating targeted therapeutic outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 16 -- Level 3 -- Community 1372
retrieved_docs.content: CARBOHYDRATE/ELECTROLYTES are classified as solutions that affect the electrolyte balance, placing them within the broader category of “Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance.” These formulations are closely linked to therapeutic mixtures that combine electrolytes with carbohydrate, antidiarrheals, and intestinal anti‑inflammatory/anti‑infective agents, reflecting their role in rehydration and gastrointestinal care. By integrating carbohydrates to enhance absorption and including agents that control diarrhea and intestinal inflammation or infection, CARBOHYDRATE/ELECTROLYTES serve as multifunctional solutions designed to restore fluid and electrolyte homeostasis while addressing common gastrointestinal disturbances.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 1 -- Level 2 -- Community 373
retrieved_docs.content: Magnesium citrate is an osmotic laxative that may be used to treat إِمْساك, but its administration is contraindicated in patients with ثقب معوي because it can worsen a perforated intestine. إِمْساك is also clinically associated with تَقَيُّء, indicating that constipation and diarrhea often occur together in functional bowel disorders. When considering magnesium citrate for symptom relief, clinicians must ensure the absence of ثقب معوي and be aware of the potential shift from إِمْساك to تَقَيُّء, tailoring treatment to maintain safe and effective gastrointestinal management.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 6 -- Level 2 -- Community 1079
retrieved_docs.content: CARBOHYDRATE/ELECTROLYTES is identified as a member of Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance, indicating that it is a specific type of solution that influences electrolyte homeostasis. Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance are classified as iv solutions used in parenteral administration of fluids, electrolytes and nutrients, meaning they are administered intravenously to provide essential fluids, electrolytes, and nutritional support. These iv solutions are encompassed within the broader category of BLOOD SUBSTITUTES AND PERFUSION SOLUTIONS, which also includes blood and related products as a sub‑category. In addition, CARBOHYDRATE/ELECTROHYDRATES has a direct relationship with ELECTROLYTES WITH CARBOHYDRATES, ANTIDIARRHEALS, INTESTINAL ANTIINFLAMMATORY/ANTIINFECTIVE AGENTS, linking it to formulations that combine electrolytes and carbohydrates and that may contain antidiarrheal and intestinal anti‑inflammatory or anti‑infective agents. Together, these relationships illustrate a hierarchical structure in which carbohydrate‑electrolyte solutions are specialized electrolyte‑balancing iv fluids that fall under the larger umbrella of blood substitutes and perfusion solutions, while also being associated with therapeutic agents aimed at managing diarrheal and intestinal inflammatory conditions.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 29 -- Level 1 -- Community 47

Salt irrigating solutions are specifically identified as members of the sodium bicarbonate group, underscoring their use in medical irrigation procedures; other irrigating solutions include glycin as a constituent. Within i.v. solution additives, the “Other i.v. solution additives” subgroup comprises the tromethamin group (e.g., dexketoprofen trometamol) and formulations that affect electrolyte balance. Solutions affecting electrolyte balance and solutions producing osmotic diuresis are subclasses of parenteral fluid, electrolyte, and nutrient administrations; the former further contains the therapeutic grouping “CARBOHYDRATE/ELECTROLYTES,” which is linked to “ELECTROLYTES WITH CARBOHYDRATES, ANTIDIARRHEALS, INTESTINAL ANTIINFLAMMATORY/ANTIINFECTIVE AGENTS,” reflecting the common practice of combining electrolytes with carbohydrate sources and agents for diarrheal or intestinal

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 17 -- Level 3 -- Community 2237
retrieved_docs.content: Blood substitutes and perfusion solutions constitute a specific category of intravenous (IV) solutions that are administered parenterally to deliver fluids, electrolytes, and nutrients. As a subclass of IV solutions used for parenteral administration, these products are formulated to replace or augment blood components, provide rapid volume expansion, and supply essential nutrients when oral intake is not possible. They play a critical role in clinical settings such as fluid resuscitation for hypovolemia, management of blood loss, and provision of nutritional support for patients who require intravenous therapy.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 8 -- Level 2 -- Community 402
retrieved_docs.content: ANTIDIARRHEALS, INTESTINAL ANTIINFLAMMATORY/ANTIINFECTIVE AGENTS is classified as a type of ANTIPROPULSIVES, indicating that these agents work by reducing intestinal motility to control diarrhea. Within the ATC (Anatomical Therapeutic Chemical) system, this group is also a subclass of OTHER ANTIDIARRHEALS in ATC, placing it among the broader category of antidiarrheal medications. In addition, ANTIDIARRHEALS, INTESTINAL ANTIINFLAMMATORY/ANTIINFECTIVE AGENTS is considered a member of the combined formulation ELECTROLYTES WITH CARBOHYDRATES, ANTIDIARRHEALS, INTESTINAL ANTIINFLAMMATORY/ANTIINFECTIVE AGENTS, which integrates antidiarrheal and intestinal anti‑inflammatory/anti‑infective agents with electrolyte and carbohydrate solutions. The entity CARBOHYDRATE/ELECTROLYTES has a direct relationship with this combined formulation, highlighting that carbohydrate‑electrolyte solutions are a key component of these multi‑ingredient products. Together, these relationships illustrate how antidiarrheal and intestinal anti‑inflammatory therapies are often co‑formulated with electrolyte and carbohydrate repletion to manage diarrheal disease, restore fluid and electrolyte balance, and improve patient outcomes.

retrieved_docs.title: UMLS -- Run 30 -- Level 1 -- Community 177

The category **CARBOHYDRATE/ELECTROLYTES** is linked by a “has relationship” to the combined **ELECTROLYTES WITH CARBOHYDRATES** class and is a member of **Solutions affecting the electrolyte balance**, which includes **IV solutions** used for parenteral fluid, electrolyte, and nutrient replacement. Collectively, these entities form a structured network of antidiarrheal and intestinal protective agents—ranging from probiotic microorganisms and metal‑based preparations to electrolyte solutions and combination therapies—that support comprehensive patient care in the prevention and treatment of diarrheal and related gastrointestinal conditions.
